event_name: UFC Fight Night: Fiziev vs. Torres

fights.fighter_a_bio: Fiziev relies on volume striking and footwork to control range, but struggles when opponents close the distance and drag him into tight exchanges. His defensive gaps compound when he's forced to wrestle—that's where opponents have found the clearest path to beating him.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Torres is a heavy favorite here because he dictates pace through aggressive pressure and clinch work, breaking opponents down over three rounds rather than seeking quick finishes. The model gives him 95% to win because Fiziev's footwork typically fails against stalking, relentless forward pressure.
fights.watch_for: Can Fiziev create space and land clean combinations before Torres locks him against the cage? If Torres establishes the clinch and controls rounds early, Fiziev's chances evaporate fast.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Shara Magomedov is a heavy-handed striker who walks opponents down with straight shots and body work, rarely getting fancy. He's rated an 83% favorite because he's proven in the cage (1557 Elo) and dictates distance on his terms.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Michel Pereira throws creative striking combos and hunts submissions, but his offense is streaky and he can get caught chasing instead of defending. He's the 17% underdog here because Magomedov's directness typically punishes fighters who improvise.
fights.watch_for: If Pereira can stay off the fence and make Magomedov miss early, he buys time to land a takedown or scramble for a submission—his only realistic path. If he eats jabs trying to load up flashy counters, Magomedov walks through and finishes him.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Sadykhov mixes strikes and takedowns effectively, using footwork to set up combinations while staying ready to shoot. His 1546 Elo shows proven success at this level, and our model favors him 69% to impose his pace and control.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Camilo hunts for wrestling exchanges and looks to drag opponents down early. He's competitive but faces a significant gap in proven output—our model gives him just 31% to get inside on someone who can pace and defend the shot.
fights.watch_for: Can Camilo land a takedown in the first round before Sadykhov's footwork and distance control take over the fight?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Almabayev hunts takedowns relentlessly and controls opponents on the mat with top pressure—he's at his best grinding out rounds from inside position. His weakness is getting there: his striking is basic, and he can be caught lunging for legs against fighters who time their counters.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Johnson mixes striking with clinch work and isn't afraid to go to the ground, but he's not a submission specialist—he uses grappling to reset and create openings. Our model gives him a slight edge at 52.5%, likely because his ability to operate in multiple phases gives him more ways to win.
fights.watch_for: Can Almabayev land a takedown in the first two rounds before Johnson's footwork and spacing keep him at distance? If the fight stays standing past round 2, Johnson's ability to strike and retreat usually decides it.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Aliskerov hunts the takedown early and leans on top control to wear opponents down—he's a grinder who uses size and wrestling to dictate the pace. His Elo rating (1540.8) reflects proven wins at this level, and our model favors him heavily (78.9%) because his grappling advantage is significant here.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Ferreira relies on striking exchanges and lateral movement to stay dangerous, but he's vulnerable to wrestlers who can close the distance and smother his offense. Despite a solid Elo (1544.7), he's a significant underdog because Aliskerov's wrestling gameplan directly counters his style.
fights.watch_for: First 5 minutes: if Aliskerov successfully lands a takedown and holds position, Ferreira's chances collapse. If Ferreira keeps the fight standing and lands clean strikes early, he can build momentum—but he'll need to stay mobile and avoid the clinch.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Magomedov is a heavy-handed wrestler who hunts takedowns relentlessly and punishes opponents on the mat with ground-and-pound. His cardio lets him maintain pressure for three rounds, and our model gives him a dominant 92.7% edge here.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Oleksiejczuk throws volume striking from range but struggles to stop committed takedown attempts. He's most dangerous when he can keep the fight standing and land combinations, but that's a tough ask against a wrestler of Magomedov's caliber.
fights.watch_for: Whether Oleksiejczuk can stuff the initial takedown attempts and make Magomedov respect his striking enough to keep distance—if he gets dragged down early, the ground control likely decides the fight.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Farman Hasanov is a slight favorite according to our model (52.3%), though Vegas hasn't priced this fight yet. Limited fight tape makes him hard to read, but his Elo rating suggests he's faced stiffer competition than Nolan.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Eric Nolan sits just behind Hasanov in our projection at 47.7%, with a nearly identical Elo rating that signals this is genuinely close to a coin flip. The lack of Vegas odds confirms both fighters are relative unknowns at this level.
fights.watch_for: Watch whether either fighter can establish their jab early and control the center—with both fighters unpredictable (chaos index 0.5 each), whoever lands cleaner volume in round one likely dictates the pace of this toss-up.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Yakhyaev is a relatively unproven fighter sitting right at the middle of the pack in terms of track record. Our model gives him nearly even odds, suggesting he'll either need to land his key weapons early or survive a full fight to grind out a decision.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Walker has a razor-thin edge in our model's eyes (50.2%) and holds a marginally stronger resume based on his Elo rating. He's positioned as the slight favorite, but this is essentially a coin flip—execution matters more than pre-fight hype.
fights.watch_for: Whether Walker can control the pace and impose his game plan for 15 minutes, or whether Yakhyaev catches him with something explosive in round one or two when both fighters are still fresh and hunting.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Naurdiev lands heavy hands early and often—he's at his most dangerous in the first two rounds when he's fresh and aggressive. The model heavily favors him (82.3%) because he controls the pace with constant pressure and doesn't let opponents settle into their game.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Vettori is a grinder who wears opponents down over three rounds with relentless wrestling and clinch work. He's the slight underdog here despite a higher Elo rating, suggesting the model thinks Naurdiev's early aggression will be too much before Vettori's cardio advantage kicks in.
fights.watch_for: Whether Vettori can survive Naurdiev's first-round onslaught and get the fight to the mat—if he does, Vettori's wrestling control and volume punching from top position could flip this fight despite the odds.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ruziboev is a heavy pressure fighter who drives forward with strikes and clinch work, forcing opponents into corners where he controls with knees and short elbows. His Elo of 1547 shows consistent wins against real competition, and our model gives him 79.5% to finish this fight as the clear favorite.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Pulyaev mixes takedown attempts with stand-up exchanges but lacks the consistent output to hold dominance—he tends to get outpaced when opponents match his pace for three rounds. At 1501 Elo and 20.5% win odds, he needs to land something clean early or he'll lose a scrambling fight.
fights.watch_for: Whether Pulyaev can create space with leg kicks or clinch breaks in round one; if Ruziboev locks him against the cage and lands sustained elbows from top position, Pulyaev's cardio usually fades and the fight's over.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Donchenko is a heavy hitter who lives in the pocket and trades power for volume—he'll walk through jabs to land his own shots but can be drawn into messy exchanges where his defense lapses. His wrestling is functional but not a primary threat, so he needs to stay aggressive and in his range to impose his will.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Gustafsson operates with better footwork and distance control, using lateral movement to create angles while staying just outside of Donchenko's power range. He mixes in takedown attempts to disrupt rhythm, and our model gives him a slight edge (50.6%) because that layered approach frustrates pure strikers.
fights.watch_for: Whether Donchenko can cut off the cage and trap Gustafsson into a phone booth brawl—if Gustafsson controls the distance and keeps circling, Donchenko's gas tank becomes the problem.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Almakhan is a heavy striker who hunts knockouts with leg kicks and hard hands, then pivots to wrestling when opponents crumble or get desperate. Our model gives him 84% to win because he controls pace and punishes hesitation—he's the clear favorite here.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Matsumoto has a higher Elo rating but comes in as a big underdog, likely because he relies on counters and timing rather than forcing the action. He needs clean openings to work; against someone as aggressive as Almakhan, he'll be backpedaling.
fights.watch_for: Does Matsumoto's counter-striking find Almakhan's chin early, or does Almakhan's leg kicks and forward pressure lock down the fight by round 2?
